humanities_role_explanation: Uses parole hearing transcripts (humanities content) to evaluate the linguistic, rhetorical, and stylistic mimicry capabilities of LLMs (humanities domain of Language and Linguistics).
benchmark_modalities_explanation: Evaluates LLM text generation capabilities based on textual transcript inputs.
analytical_tasks_explanation: Evaluates the capacity of LLMs to replicate speaker-specific rhetorical fingerprints across multiple dimensions including sentence complexity, lexical diversity, discourse markers, nominalizations, modal verbs, and pronoun usage profiles.

general_purpose_benchmark.note: It is a domain-specific evaluation dataset and study focusing on rhetorical and stylistic impersonation in legal hearings, rather than a general-purpose benchmark.

evaluates_generative_llm.note: Evaluates GPT-4o, GPT-4.1, and DeepSeek R1.

llm_is_primary_system.note: LLMs are the primary systems being tested on their stylistic generation limits.

arts_humanities_data.note: Mapped to Language and Linguistics (focusing on rhetoric, stylistics, and spoken discourse analysis).

quantitative_metrics.note: Evaluates using z-scores of linguistic features, Measure of Textual Lexical Diversity (MTLD), and syntactic/dependency-based complexity measurements.

english_language.note: The paper and the evaluated PSH v1.0 corpus are in English.

summary: This paper evaluates the capacity of state-of-the-art LLMs to impersonate specific human speakers' rhetorical styles in a high-stakes legal context (California parole hearings). By extracting multi-dimensional linguistic fingerprints of four presiding commissioners, the authors test whether explicit rhetorical guidance in prompts improves stylistic alignment compared to corpus-based priming. The study finds that LLMs fail to fully replicate individual human stylistic profiles, as they are ultimately dominated by their own model-specific stylistic fingerprints.
key_findings: All tested LLMs fail to match human speaker-specific stylistic signatures, instead reverting to their own model-specific fingerprints. Across all prompting conditions, LLMs show high resistance to adapting spoken registers, exhibiting massive overuses of nominalizations, excessively complex sentences, and significantly higher lexical diversity (z-scores up to 40.20) compared to natural human speech, likely due to a strong bias toward their formal written training data.
